summary: Reynoldstown finally sees movement on that long-stalled BeltLine corner project while Decatur unveils its affordable housing village. Meanwhile, airport chaos continues as ICE agents patrol Hartsfield-Jackson amid ongoing TSA shortages.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Stalled BeltLine Corner Project Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting empty, that cleared corner lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace is finally showing signs of life. Embry Development has started construction on 85 residential units about a block from the BeltLine. Anyone who walks the Eastside Trail regularly has been wondering what was happening with this prime spot.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the BeltLine Eastside Trail recently, you've probably noticed the construction activity at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown. After years of being an empty lot (the buildings were demolished back in 2021), this corner is finally getting the development it was promised.

Embry Development Company took over this project from Toll Brothers two years ago, and they're moving forward with 85 residential units just a block west of the BeltLine. The original clearing happened under Toll Brothers, but the project sat dormant until Embry stepped in. For neighbors who've been staring at dirt for half a decade, this is welcome news.

The location is prime real estate for anyone wanting BeltLine access without the premium prices you see closer to Ponce City Market. It's one of those spots that feels like the old industrial Reynoldstown but with easy access to the trail and Memorial Drive. The development should help bridge that gap between the established BeltLine corridor and the residential streets that make this neighborhood special.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown showing early development work

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Opens Next Week
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching its soft opening with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. With median home values hitting $700K+ in Decatur proper, this pocket neighborhood on Midway Road aims to offer more accessible ITP living. It's the kind of development that could serve as a model for other areas struggling with affordability.
stories.body:
Decatur has been feeling the pinch of rising home prices (we're talking $700K+ median values), so the upcoming launch of Cottages at Midway feels like a breath of fresh air. This isn't your typical subdivision. Instead, 18 standalone houses are arranged around shared courtyards and greenspace on a 2.1-acre site that used to be underused lots.

The concept here is 'missing middle' housing, which is planning-speak for the kinds of homes that fall between apartments and McMansions. Think smaller footprints but with the privacy of a detached home, plus shared amenities that make the community feel more connected. The location on Midway Road puts you close enough to downtown Decatur to bike there but far enough out to avoid some of the premium pricing.

For anyone watching Atlanta's housing crisis unfold, this project represents one potential solution. It's denser than typical suburban development but maintains the character that makes people want to live ITP. Whether this model can scale to other neighborhoods remains to be seen, but it's encouraging to see developers trying something different than the usual luxury tower approach.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of Cottages at Midway development with houses arranged around shared courtyards

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The Gordon White Parkside project is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to a stretch of the BeltLine that doesn't see a lot of new residential development. Ackerman & Co. (the Lee + White folks) started this project a year ago, and the first seven units should hit the market this spring. It's rare to see townhomes along the southwest BeltLine corridor.
stories.body:
Drive down Hopkins Street in the West End and you'll see the early frames of what's becoming Gordon White Parkside, formerly known as Hopkins and White Townhomes. Ackerman & Co., the same developer behind the Lee + White complex, cleared this site a year ago and is now putting up 23 townhomes along the BeltLine's southwest corridor.

What makes this project interesting is its location. This stretch of the BeltLine doesn't see nearly as much residential development as the eastside trail, partly because the surrounding neighborhoods have been slower to gentrify. The townhomes represent a different approach to BeltLine development, focusing on for-sale units rather than the rental complexes that dominate other sections.

The first seven properties should be available this spring, and they'll be a test case for whether there's demand for new construction townhomes in this part of the city. Given Ackerman's success with Lee + White's mix of office and retail, they clearly see potential in this corridor that others have overlooked.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of Gordon White Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. After years of pop-ups around the city, this is their permanent home base in one of Inman Park's quieter corners.
stories.body:
After following Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez through various pop-ups around Atlanta, it's exciting to see them finally land in a permanent spot. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, just a short walk from the Freedom Park Trail. The space gives them room to expand beyond the limited menus they've been working with at temporary locations.

The food here is what they call Asian-American comfort food, which translates to dishes like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, and those Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. The cocktail program includes Thai tea martinis and other drinks that complement the food without overwhelming it. Both chefs bring serious restaurant experience (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York), and it shows in the execution.

The location is smart too. Elizabeth Street doesn't get the foot traffic of Highland Avenue, but it's accessible to both the Freedom Park Trail and the neighborhoods around it. For Inman Park residents who want something different from the usual Highland Avenue options, this fills a real gap in the local dining scene.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter from Mushi Ni showing roasted chicken with garnishes

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Third Atlanta Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The Gansevoort Market creator is bringing Vela Park Food Works to the East Lake area, with six chef-driven concepts and outdoor dining. This eastside location joins his other Atlanta food halls. The mid-April opening will test whether the food hall concept works in this part of the city, near East Lake Golf Club.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid, the guy behind New York's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food hall this April. Vela Park Food Works sits within the new Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, bringing his multi-vendor concept to a part of the city that hasn't seen this type of dining destination.

The setup includes six different chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and plenty of outdoor dining space. Montwaid's previous Atlanta ventures have been successful, so there's reason to be optimistic about this one. The location is interesting because it's not in an obvious food hall neighborhood like Ponce City Market or Krog Street Market, but rather embedded in a residential development.

The real test will be whether the East Lake area can support this type of dining concept. It's close enough to Decatur and East Atlanta Village to draw from those communities, but it's also banking on the new residents of Vela Park and nearby neighborhoods. If it works, it could be a model for integrating food halls into residential developments rather than just commercial districts.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing multiple dining concepts and seating areas

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Austin-Style BBQ Arrives at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: John Lewis brought his Texas-style barbecue operation to the Ansley Mall location behind Cook's Warehouse, complete with a stunning upstairs smokehouse overlooking the new BeltLine pedestrian bridge. Six 22-foot smokers run 24 hours a day, producing some of Atlanta's best brisket. The setup is unlike anything you'll see at other local BBQ spots.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue is something else entirely. John Lewis (the pitmaster, not the congressman) put six 22-foot smokers on the second floor of his Ansley Mall location, with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. It looks more like a spaceship engine room than a typical BBQ kitchen, and that's exactly the point.

The smokers, which Lewis helped weld from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, run around the clock in staggered eight-hour shifts. This factory-like approach produces consistently excellent brisket that rivals anything in the city. Lewis cut his teeth in Austin and Charleston, and he's brought that level of craft and scale to Atlanta in a way that respects both traditions.

The location works perfectly for both BeltLine traffic and neighborhood diners. You can grab lunch after a run on the trail or make it a destination dinner. The proximity to Cook's Warehouse creates a little food cluster that wasn't there before, and the pedestrian bridge makes it easily accessible from the Virginia-Highland side.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Nearly Done
stories.summary: After a decade in planning and months of construction, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a car speedway to a people-friendly street is almost complete. The project includes a two-way protected bike lane, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures from Commerce Drive to the Atlanta city limits. It's the kind of infrastructure change that takes forever but makes a real difference once it's done.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue in Decatur used to be one of those streets where cars flew by at 45 mph in a 35 zone. After nearly a decade of planning and months of construction, it's becoming something much more civilized. The Complete Streets project runs from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) to the city limits at Paden Circle, right by East Lake MARTA station.

The centerpiece is a two-way protected bike lane with concrete barriers, but the project also includes new sidewalks, infrastructure upgrades, and repaving. For anyone who's tried to bike or walk along this corridor before, the difference is dramatic. The concrete barriers actually protect cyclists from traffic, rather than just hoping a painted line will keep everyone safe.

This is the kind of infrastructure investment that pays dividends for decades. Decatur has been working toward a more walkable, bikeable city for years, and projects like this are how you actually get there. It connects residential neighborhoods to downtown Decatur and provides a safer route to East Lake MARTA station for people who don't want to drive everywhere.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur with concrete barriers

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Restrictions While Pressing Mayor on Fire Contract
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions to limit ICE detention facilities and require documentation when federal agents work with Atlanta police. Meanwhile, dozens of residents showed up demanding answers about why Mayor Dickens hasn't signed the firefighters' union contract that council approved months ago. The full council votes April 20th.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee had a busy meeting, advancing two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in the city while fielding tough questions about the mayor's handling of the firefighters' union contract. The ICE-related measures would ban detention facilities within city limits and require APD to document whenever federal immigration agents are present during their operations.

The bigger drama came from the packed audience demanding answers about the firefighters' union contract. Council approved the city's first-ever firefighter union contract back in April 2025, but Mayor Dickens still hasn't signed it. Dozens of firefighters and residents showed up asking why, and the answers weren't satisfying anyone.

Both ICE resolutions passed unanimously in committee and head to the full council for an April 20th vote. The timing is notable given the current deployment of ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson airport. These local measures won't stop federal operations, but they signal where the city stands on immigration enforcement partnerships.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: ICE Agents Patrol Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA Shortage Continues
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents in tactical gear are now walking around Atlanta's airport to provide security support as TSA agents continue calling out sick during the partial government shutdown. The airport is telling travelers to arrive four hours early. Mayor Dickens says the ICE agents won't do immigration enforcement, but their presence is raising questions about the city's sanctuary policies.
stories.body:
Walking through Hartsfield-Jackson this week, you'll see something new: ICE agents in tactical gear patrolling the terminals. They're there because TSA agents are calling out sick at record rates during the partial government shutdown, and somebody needs to maintain security at the world's busiest airport. The situation is unprecedented and a bit surreal.

Mayor Dickens was quick to clarify that these agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, just general security support. But for a city that just advanced resolutions to limit ICE activities, having federal immigration agents prominently patrolling our airport sends a mixed message. The visual alone is jarring for travelers who aren't expecting to see tactical teams in the concourse.

The bigger issue is how long this can continue. TSA agents are working without pay, airport wait times are hitting four hours, and now we're relying on immigration enforcement officers to maintain basic airport security. It's a band-aid solution to a larger problem, and everyone knows it. The real question is how much longer travelers will tolerate this level of disruption before something gives.
stories.imageAlt: TSA security checkpoint at airport during government shutdown

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Crack National 'Best Places' Top 10
stories.summary: A new ranking puts Atlantic Station at #1 and Midtown at #6 on a national list of best places to live, based on schools, cost of living, jobs, and amenities. Whether you agree with the methodology or not, it's notable that 20% of the top 10 spots went to Atlanta neighborhoods. The recognition should help with economic development efforts, even if locals might question some of the criteria.
stories.body:
Niche.com's new rankings have Atlantic Station claiming the #1 spot nationally for best places to live, with Midtown coming in at #6. The methodology looks at public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. For Atlanta to claim 20% of the top 10 spots is pretty remarkable, even if you question some of the specific choices.

Atlantic Station as #1 might surprise locals who see it more as a shopping center with apartments than a neighborhood, but the ranking probably reflects its walkability, amenities, and job access. Midtown at #6 makes more sense to most people familiar with the area. Both locations offer the urban lifestyle that younger professionals are seeking, plus reasonable access to everything Atlanta has to offer.

The rankings come from FBI crime data, Census information, and CDC health metrics, so they're not just based on vibes. Whether this translates into more people moving here remains to be seen, but it's the kind of national recognition that economic development folks love to cite. And honestly, both areas have improved dramatically over the past decade, so maybe the outside perspective isn't wrong.
stories.imageAlt: Atlantic Station mixed-use development with pedestrians and retail spaces
